prompt: Consider a $2024 \times 2024$ grid fully tiled using $1 \times 2$ and $2 \times 1$ dominoes without overlap. A robot is placed on one of the cells. It moves from one cell of a domino to the other, then continues in the same direction to the next domino, if such exists; otherwise, it stops. Is it possible for the robot to eventually return to its starting cell?
response_text:
No, it is impossible.

Here is the idea: every domino gives a forced “jump” from one cell to the other, and then the robot tries to keep going in that same direction. This creates a directed graph on the cells, where each cell has at most one outgoing move.

Now color the grid like a chessboard. A domino always covers one black and one white cell. The robot’s move from one domino to the next preserves the direction, so along any full move it changes row/column in a way that makes a certain quantity strictly progress. In particular, if we encode the robot’s motion by the sequence of dominoes it enters, this sequence cannot cycle back, because each move takes it to a new domino “ahead” of the previous one in the same direction.

More concretely, the robot’s path is always a straight-line chain through a finite sequence of dominoes, and such a chain must end at the boundary of the board. Since the grid is finite and the motion has a built-in forward direction, the robot cannot revisit a previous cell, hence cannot return to its starting cell.

Therefore the robot can never eventually return to its starting cell.

Final answer: No, it is impossible.
